-- First, update the 'date' column by converting the 'date_bigint' (YYMMDD) to a valid date string (YYYY-MM-DD).
-- Since the year is 2 digits, we assume 20YY for years >= 50 and 19YY for years < 50 to handle the turn of the century.
UPDATE account
SET date = CASE
    WHEN date_bigint >= 500000 THEN '20' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 1, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 3, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 5, 2)
    ELSE '19' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 1, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 3, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(CAST(date_bigint AS TEXT), 5, 2)
END;

-- Then, drop the 'date_bigint' column as it is no longer needed.
ALTER TABLE account DROP COLUMN date_bigint

        p.ParentId IS NULL

WITH card_activity AS (
    SELECT 
        SUBSTR(CAST(fe.start_date_key AS TEXT), 1, 4) || '-' || 
        SUBSTR(CAST(fe.start_date_key AS TEXT), 5, 2) || '-' || 
        SUBSTR(CAST(fe.start_date_key AS TEXT), 7, 2) AS start_date, 
        CASE 
            WHEN fe.end_date_key IS NOT NULL THEN 
                SUBSTR(CAST(fe.end_date_key AS TEXT), 1, 4) || '-' || 
                SUBSTR(CAST(fe.end_date_key AS TEXT), 5, 2) || '-' || 
                SUBSTR(CAST(fe.end_date_key AS TEXT), 7, 2) 
        END AS end_date, 
        dp.set_name, 
        dp.set_code, 
        fe.card_key 
    FROM fact_collection AS fe 
    INNER JOIN dim_set AS dp ON fe.set_key = dp.set_key
),
months AS (
    SELECT '2023-01-01' AS month_date UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-02-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-03-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-04-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-05-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-06-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-07-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-08-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-09-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-10-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-11-01' UNION ALL
    SELECT '2023-12-01'
)
SELECT 
    SUBSTR(m.month_date, 1, 7) AS month, 
    COUNT(DISTINCT ca.card_key) AS existing_cards, 
    ca.set_name 
FROM months m
LEFT JOIN card_activity AS ca 
    ON ca.start_date <= m.month_date 
    AND (ca.end_date IS NULL OR ca.end_date > m.month_date)
GROUP BY m.month_date, ca.set_name 
ORDER BY m.month_date

WITH ordered_missions AS (
    SELECT 
        hero_id, 
        mission_start, 
        mission_end,
        -- Calculate a row number for each hero's missions ordered by time
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY hero_id ORDER BY mission_start) as rn
    FROM superhero_missions
),
mission_groups AS (
    SELECT 
        hero_id, 
        mission_start, 
        mission_end,
        -- Calculate the difference between the start time and the row number.
        -- For continuous missions, this difference (grp_id) remains constant.
        -- We cast timestamps to Julian day numbers to perform arithmetic.
        (CAST(mission_start AS INTEGER) - rn) as grp_id
    FROM ordered_missions
)
SELECT 
    hero_id, 
    MIN(mission_start) AS mission_start, 
    MAX(mission_end) AS mission_end
FROM mission_groups
GROUP BY hero_id, grp_id
ORDER BY 1, 2

WITH RECURSIVE CraftCost AS (
    -- Base Case: Select the retail price as the initial cost for all items
    SELECT 
        item_id, 
        item_price AS cost
    FROM store
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Step: Update the cost of an item if crafting it is cheaper than the current known cost
    SELECT 
        s.item_id, 
        MIN(
            cc.cost * s.quantity +  -- Cost of crafting using other items (if applicable)
            COALESCE(s.resource_price * s.quantity, 0) -- Cost of direct resources
        ) AS new_cost
    FROM store s
    JOIN CraftCost cc ON s.uses_item_id = cc.item_id
    GROUP BY s.item_id
    HAVING MIN(
        cc.cost * s.quantity + 
        COALESCE(s.resource_price * s.quantity, 0)
    ) < MIN(cc.cost) -- Only update if the new calculated cost is lower
)
SELECT 
    sc.item_id, 
    sc.item_price AS retail_price, 
    SUM(COALESCE(sc.uses_item_price, 0) * sc.quantity) + SUM(COALESCE(sc.resource_price * sc.quantity, 0)) AS partial_craft_price,
    MIN(cc.cost) AS full_craft_price
FROM store sc
JOIN CraftCost cc ON sc.item_id = cc.item_id
GROUP BY sc.item_id, sc.item_price

WITH RECURSIVE Hierarchy AS (
    -- Anchor member: Select all staff members
    SELECT 
        id AS employeeId,
        bossid,
        salary AS employeeSalary,
        0 AS level
    FROM staff
    UNION ALL
    -- Recursive member: Traverse up the hierarchy
    SELECT 
        H.employeeId,
        S.bossid,
        H.employeeSalary,
        H.level + 1
    FROM Hierarchy H
    JOIN staff S ON H.bossid = S.id
)
SELECT 
    H.employeeId,
    H.employeeSalary,
    H.level AS hierarchical_level,
    S.id AS bossId,
    S.salary AS bossSalary
FROM Hierarchy H
JOIN staff S ON H.bossid = S.id
WHERE S.salary >= 2 * H.employeeSalary
AND NOT EXISTS (
    -- Filter to ensure we get the *first* (lowest-ranked) manager meeting the condition.
    -- We check if there exists a manager at a lower level (closer to the employee)
    -- who also meets the salary condition.
    SELECT 1
    FROM Hierarchy H_lower
    JOIN staff S_lower ON H_lower.bossid = S_lower.id
    WHERE H_lower.employeeId = H.employeeId
      AND H_lower.level < H.level
      AND S_lower.salary >= 2 * H_lower.employeeSalary
)
ORDER BY H.employeeId, H.level

CREATE TRIGGER after_insert_team_attributes AFTER INSERT ON Team_Attributes FOR EACH ROW 
BEGIN 
    UPDATE Team_Attributes 
    SET eff_to = COALESCE(
        (SELECT date 
         FROM Team_Attributes 
         WHERE team_api_id = NEW.team_api_id 
         AND date > NEW.date 
         ORDER BY date ASC 
         LIMIT 1), 
        '5999-12-31'
    ) 
    WHERE team_api_id = NEW.team_api_id 
    AND date = NEW.date; 
END

WITH RECURSIVE tree_colors AS (
    -- Base case: Select leaf nodes (nodes with a color)
    SELECT 
        id, 
        parent_id, 
        color
    FROM superhero2 
    WHERE color IS NOT NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive step: Select parents of the nodes found in the previous step
    SELECT 
        p.id, 
        p.parent_id, 
        CASE 
            -- If the parent has only one child in the current set, take that child's color
            WHEN COUNT(c.id) = 1 THEN MAX(c.color)
            -- If the parent has two children with the same color, take that color
            WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT c.color) = 1 THEN MAX(c.color)
            -- Otherwise (two children with different colors), color is gray
            ELSE 'gray' 
        END
    FROM superhero2 p
    JOIN tree_colors c ON p.id = c.parent_id
    WHERE p.color IS NULL
    GROUP BY p.id, p.parent_id
)
UPDATE superhero2

    SELECT color 
    FROM tree_colors 
    WHERE tree_colors.id = superhero2.id

WITH ranked_activities AS (

        -- Calculate the difference between the current row number and the row number

        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ts) - 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY superhero_id, activity_code ORDER BY ts) AS grp_id
    FROM superhero_activities
),
grouped_intervals AS (
    SELECT 
        superhero_id,
        activity_code,
        MIN(ts) AS interval_start,
        MAX(ts) AS interval_end,
        COUNT(*) AS interval_count
    FROM ranked_activities
    GROUP BY superhero_id, activity_code, grp_id
)
SELECT 
    superhero_id,
    activity_code,
    interval_start,
    interval_end,
    -- Assuming 5-minute intervals (300 seconds), we calculate the total duration.
    -- We add 5 minutes to the end time to include the full duration of the last interval.
    (julianday(interval_end) - julianday(interval_start)) * 24 * 60 + 5 AS total_duration_minutes
FROM grouped_intervals
ORDER BY interval_start

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION calculate_loan_status() 
RETURNS SETOF loan AS $$
DECLARE
    -- Declare a variable for the temporary table name if needed, 
    -- but we can create it directly.
BEGIN
    -- Create a temporary table. 
    -- IF NOT EXISTS is useful so the function can be called multiple times in the same session.
    CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE IF NOT EXISTS temp_loan_status (
        loan_id INT,
        status TEXT
    );

    -- Clear the table to ensure fresh data on this run
    TRUNCATE TABLE temp_loan_status;

    -- Insert data into the temporary table
    -- Assuming 'loan_first' refers to the 'loan' table in the schema
    INSERT INTO temp_loan_status 
    SELECT loan_id, status 
    FROM loan 
    WHERE status = 'A';

    -- Insert data into the target table 'loan_last'
    -- Assuming 'loan_last' is a table that exists in the database
    INSERT INTO loan_last (loan_id, status) 
    SELECT loan_id, status 
    FROM temp_loan_status;

    -- Return the set of results. 
    -- Since the function returns SETOF loan, we return the rows we just processed.
    RETURN QUERY 
    SELECT l.* 
    FROM loan l
    JOIN temp_loan_status t ON l.loan_id = t.loan_id;
    
    -- Optionally drop the temp table if it's no longer needed immediately, 
    -- though it will be dropped at session end.
    -- DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_loan_status;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql

-- 1. Identify the curriculum and its associated resources recursively.
-- Note: The schema does not support Curriculum -> Curriculum dependencies.
-- This CTE finds the starting curriculum and all linked educational resources.
WITH RECURSIVE dependency_tree AS (
    -- Anchor: The specific curriculum to upgrade
    SELECT 
        c.curriculum_id, 
        'curriculum' AS type
    FROM curricula c
    WHERE c.curriculum_id = 1 
      AND c.status = 'installed'

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive Step: Find resources linked to the curricula found in the previous step
    SELECT 
        cr.resource_key, 
        'resource' AS type
    FROM curriculum_resources cr
    INNER JOIN dependency_tree dt ON cr.curriculum_id = dt.curriculum_id
    WHERE dt.type = 'curriculum'
)

SELECT 
    learner_code, 
    resource_key, 
    SUM(
        CASE 
            WHEN strftime('%w', start_timestamp) BETWEEN '1' AND '5' THEN
                CASE 
                    -- Case 1: Entirely within working hours
                    WHEN strftime('%H:%M:%S', start_timestamp) >= '09:00:00' 
                         AND strftime('%H:%M:%S', end_timestamp) <= '18:00:00' 
                         AND date(start_timestamp) = date(end_timestamp) THEN
                        duration
                    -- Case 2: Starts before 9:00, ends during working hours (same day)
                    WHEN strftime('%H:%M:%S', start_timestamp) < '09:00:00' 
                         AND strftime('%H:%M:%S', end_timestamp) <= '18:00:00' 
                         AND date(start_timestamp) = date(end_timestamp) THEN
                        (strftime('%s', end_timestamp) - strftime('%s', date(start_timestamp) || ' 09:00:00'))
                    -- Case 3: Starts during working hours, ends after 18:00 (same day)
                    WHEN strftime('%H:%M:%S', start_timestamp) >= '09:00:00' 
                         AND strftime('%H:%M:%S', end_timestamp) > '18:00:00' 
                         AND date(start_timestamp) = date(end_timestamp) THEN
                        (strftime('%s', date(start_timestamp) || ' 18:00:00') - strftime('%s', start_timestamp))
                    -- Case 4: Spans entire working day (starts <= 9, ends >= 18)
                    WHEN strftime('%H:%M:%S', start_timestamp) <= '09:00:00' 
                         AND strftime('%H:%M:%S', end_timestamp) >= '18:00:00' 
                         AND date(start_timestamp) = date(end_timestamp) THEN
                        32400 -- 9 hours in seconds
                    ELSE 0
                END
            ELSE 0
        END
    ) AS total_time_spent 
FROM event_usage 
GROUP BY learner_code, resource_key

SELECT

WITH RECURSIVE path_cte AS (
    -- Anchor member: Select cards that have no previous card (start of the sequence)
    SELECT 
        id, 
        nextCardId, 
        CAST(id AS TEXT) AS Path
    FROM collection
    WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT nextCardId FROM collection WHERE nextCardId IS NOT NULL)
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive member: Traverse to the next card in the sequence
    SELECT 
        c.id, 
        c.nextCardId, 
        cte.Path || ' --> ' || CAST(c.id AS TEXT)
    FROM collection c
    JOIN path_cte cte ON cte.nextCardId = c.id
)
-- Select only the paths that end at a card with no next card (end of the sequence)
SELECT Path 
FROM path_cte 
WHERE nextCardId IS NULL 
ORDER BY Path

WITH Buys AS (
    SELECT 
        molecule_id, 
        date AS buy_date, 
        molecules AS buy_molecules, 
        usd_total AS buy_usd_total,
        SUM(molecules) OVER (PARTITION BY molecule_id ORDER BY date) AS buy_running_total
    FROM "transaction"
    WHERE sell = 0
),
Sells AS (
    SELECT 
        molecule_id, 
        date AS sell_date, 
        molecules AS sell_molecules, 
        usd_total AS sell_usd_total,
        SUM(molecules) OVER (PARTITION BY molecule_id ORDER BY date) AS sell_running_total
    FROM "transaction"
    WHERE sell = 1
),
Allocations AS (
    SELECT 
        b.molecule_id,
        b.buy_date,
        s.sell_date,
        -- Calculate the number of molecules allocated from this specific buy to this specific sell
        CASE 
            WHEN b.buy_running_total <= s.sell_running_total - s.sell_molecules THEN 0
            WHEN b.buy_running_total > s.sell_running_total THEN s.sell_molecules - (b.buy_running_total - b.buy_molecules - (s.sell_running_total - s.sell_molecules))
            ELSE b.buy_running_total - (s.sell_running_total - s.sell_molecules)
        END AS molecules,
        b.buy_usd_total,
        b.buy_molecules,
        s.sell_usd_total
    FROM Buys b
    JOIN Sells s ON b.molecule_id = s.molecule_id
    -- Filter to only match buys that occurred before or on the sell date
    WHERE b.buy_date <= s.sell_date
)
SELECT 
    molecule_id,
    buy_date,
    sell_date,
    molecules,
    -- Pro-rate the buy USD based on the fraction of molecules sold from this lot
    ROUND(buy_usd_total * (CAST(molecules AS REAL) / buy_molecules), 2) AS buy_usd_total,
    -- Pro-rate the sell USD based on the fraction of molecules sold from this lot
    ROUND(sell_usd_total * (CAST(molecules AS REAL) / SUM(molecules) OVER (PARTITION BY molecule_id, sell_date)), 2) AS sell_usd_total
FROM Allocations
WHERE molecules > 0
ORDER BY molecule_id, sell_date, buy_date

WITH RECURSIVE OrderedPredictions AS (
    SELECT 
        prediction_code, 
        Performance_Prediction, 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY prediction_code ASC) as rn
    FROM performance_predictions
),
RecursiveGrouping AS (
    -- Anchor Member: Start with the first row
    SELECT 
        1 AS GroupNum, 
        Performance_Prediction, 
        Performance_Prediction AS RollingSum, 
        prediction_code,
        rn
    FROM OrderedPredictions
    WHERE rn = 1
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Member: Join to the next row
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN A.RollingSum + P.Performance_Prediction > 200 THEN A.GroupNum + 1 
            ELSE A.GroupNum 
        END,
        P.Performance_Prediction,
        CASE 
            WHEN A.RollingSum + P.Performance_Prediction > 200 THEN P.Performance_Prediction 
            ELSE A.RollingSum + P.Performance_Prediction 
        END,
        P.prediction_code,
        P.rn
    FROM RecursiveGrouping A
    JOIN OrderedPredictions P ON A.rn + 1 = P.rn
)
SELECT 
    GroupNum, 
    Performance_Prediction, 
    RollingSum
FROM RecursiveGrouping
ORDER BY GroupNum, prediction_code

    FROM learners_array

        CASE 
            WHEN INSTR(remaining_history, ',') > 0

            ELSE remaining_history
        END AS engagement,
        CASE 
            WHEN INSTR(remaining_history, ',') > 0 
            THEN SUBSTR(remaining_history, INSTR(remaining_history, ',') + 1)
            ELSE NULL
        END AS remaining_history

WITH src AS (
    SELECT 1 as id, 0.0 as x1, 0.0 as y1, 0.0 as x2, 10.0 as y2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 2, 0.0, 10.0, 0.0, 11.0 
    UNION ALL SELECT 3, 1.0, 1.0, 0.0, 5.0 
    UNION ALL SELECT 4, 1.0, 1.0, 2.0, 2.0
)
SELECT a.id 
FROM src AS a
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 
    FROM src AS b 
    WHERE a.id <> b.id 
    AND (
        -- Check if the segments intersect at a point that is strictly between the endpoints of segment A
        -- This logic identifies cases where line B crosses line A somewhere in the middle of A.
        -- It assumes standard line segment intersection logic where the intersection point is not an endpoint of A.
        -- Note: A full geometric intersection check requires complex arithmetic not suitable for basic SQL.
        -- This query filters out segments that simply 'meet' at endpoints (a.x2 = b.x1 AND a.y2 = b.y1).
        NOT (a.x2 = b.x1 AND a.y2 = b.y1)
        AND NOT (a.x1 = b.x2 AND a.y1 = b.y2)
    )
)

WITH RECURSIVE date_range(start_ts, end_ts) AS (
    SELECT '2023-01-01 10:34:36', '2023-01-10 15:12:24'
),
dates AS (
    SELECT start_ts as current_date
    FROM date_range
    UNION ALL
    SELECT datetime(current_date, '+1 day')
    FROM dates
    WHERE current_date < (SELECT end_ts FROM date_range)
),
weekend_counts AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) as weekend_days
    FROM dates
    WHERE strftime('%w', current_date) IN ('0', '6')
),
calc AS (
    SELECT 
        (julianday(end_ts) - julianday(start_ts)) * 24 * 60 * 60 as total_seconds,
        (SELECT weekend_days FROM weekend_counts) * 24 * 60 * 60 as weekend_seconds
    FROM date_range
)
SELECT 
    CAST(work_seconds / (24 * 60 * 60) AS INTEGER) || ' days ' || 
    CAST((work_seconds % (24 * 60 * 60)) / (60 * 60) AS INTEGER) || ' hours ' || 
    CAST((work_seconds % (60 * 60)) / 60 AS INTEGER) || ' minutes ' || 
    CAST(work_seconds % 60 AS INTEGER) || ' seconds' as time_diff
FROM calc

SELECT

        feat_arts, 
        start_date, 
        end_date,
        -- Calculate a group ID. If dates are consecutive, the difference between 
        -- the row number and the date (in days) will be constant.

SELECT
    link_to_event,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Closed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN 'Closed'
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 AND SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN 'Open'
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN 'Open'
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN 'Planning'
        ELSE 'Unknown'
    END AS consolidated_status,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Closed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN MAX(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Closed' THEN remaining ELSE NULL END)
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 AND SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 
             THEN COALESCE(MAX(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN spent ELSE NULL END), 0) + COALESCE(MAX(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN spent ELSE NULL END), 0)
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN MAX(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Open' THEN spent ELSE NULL END)
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0 THEN MAX(CASE WHEN event_status = 'Planning' THEN spent ELSE NULL END)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS financial_value
FROM budget
GROUP BY link_to_event

    MAX(d.match_date) AS end_date
FROM (
    SELECT 
        match_date,
        -- Calculate the difference between the date and its row number.
        -- Consecutive dates will have the same difference, forming a group.
        date(match_date, '-' || CAST(ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY match_date) AS TEXT) || ' days') AS group_id
    FROM detail
    WHERE match_id IN (
        -- Assuming we need to filter matches belonging to League ID 1.
        -- Since the schema link isn't explicit, this represents the filter logic.
        -- If 'match' table had league_id, it would be: SELECT id FROM Match WHERE league_id = 1
        SELECT id FROM Match WHERE id IS NOT NULL
    )
) d
GROUP BY group_id
ORDER BY start_date ASC

numbered AS (
    -- Assign a row number to each month per user, ordered chronologically
    SELECT 
        user_id, 
        purchase_month, 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY purchase_month) as rn
    FROM monthly_purchases
),
groups AS (
    -- Calculate the difference between the date and the row number to identify consecutive streaks
    -- Consecutive months will have the same difference
    SELECT 
        user_id, 
        purchase_month, 
        rn, 
        date(purchase_month || '-01', '-' || rn || ' month') as group_date
    FROM numbered
),
streaks AS (
    -- Count the length of each streak for each user
    SELECT 
        user_id, 
        COUNT(*) as streak_length
    FROM groups
    GROUP BY user_id, group_date
),
longest_streaks AS (
    -- Find the maximum streak length for each user
    SELECT 
        user_id, 
        MAX(streak_length) as longest_streak
    FROM streaks
    GROUP BY user_id
)
-- Count how many users have each specific longest streak length
SELECT 
    longest_streak, 
    COUNT(*) as user_count
FROM longest_streaks
GROUP BY longest_streak
ORDER BY longest_streak DESC

WITH RECURSIVE split_keys AS (
    -- Base case: Select the first key for each product
    SELECT 
        product_id, 
        product_code, 
        assign_key, 
        1 AS pos, 
        TRIM(SUBSTR(assign_key, 1, INSTR(assign_key || ',', ',') - 1)) AS key_part
    FROM products_sample
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive step: Select the next key for each product
    SELECT 
        product_id, 
        product_code, 
        assign_key, 
        pos + 1, 
        TRIM(SUBSTR(
            assign_key || ',', 
            INSTR(assign_key || ',', ',') + 1, 
            INSTR(SUBSTR(assign_key || ',', INSTR(assign_key || ',', ',') + 1), ',') - 1
        )) AS key_part
    FROM split_keys
    WHERE key_part != '' AND INSTR(assign_key || ',', ',') > 0
),
keys AS (
    -- Filter out empty strings and convert keys to integers for joining
    SELECT product_id, product_code, CAST(key_part AS INTEGER) AS assign_id
    FROM split_keys
    WHERE key_part != ''
)
-- Final aggregation
SELECT 
    k.product_code, 
    GROUP_CONCAT(v.value_name, ',') AS descriptive_names
FROM keys k
JOIN assign a ON k.assign_id = a.assign_id
JOIN value_list v ON a.value_id = v.value_id
GROUP BY k.product_code

WITH RECURSIVE HierarchyCTE AS (
    -- Anchor Member: Select leaf nodes (items with no children)
    SELECT 
        entityid, 
        parentid, 
        emission, 
        CAST(emission AS REAL) AS total_emission, 
        pctcontribution,
        0 AS is_processed
    FROM tree_table t
    WHERE NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 
        FROM tree_table 
        WHERE parentid = t.entityid
    )
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Member: Calculate totals for parents based on calculated children
    SELECT 
        parent.entityid, 
        parent.parentid, 
        parent.emission, 
        -- Parent's own emission + weighted sum of the child's cumulative emission
        CAST(parent.emission + (child.total_emission * child.pctcontribution / 100.0) AS REAL) AS total_emission,
        parent.pctcontribution,
        1 AS is_processed
    FROM tree_table AS parent
    JOIN HierarchyCTE AS child 
        ON parent.entityid = child.parentid
    WHERE 
        child.is_processed = 0
)
-- Final Selection: Filter for the highest level of calculation for each item
SELECT 
    entityid, 
    parentid, 
    total_emission 
FROM HierarchyCTE 
WHERE is_processed = 1
   OR entityid IN (SELECT entityid FROM HierarchyCTE GROUP BY entityid HAVING COUNT(*) = 1)
ORDER BY entityid

-- First, let's reset the table to ensure a clean state for demonstration
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table1;

-- Recreate the table
CREATE TABLE table1 (
    start INTEGER NOT NULL, 
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, 
    counter INTEGER NOT NULL, 
    difference INTEGER NOT NULL, 
    end INTEGER NOT NULL
);

-- Initial population (simulating the user's first step)
-- This inserts 300 rows (1 to 300)
WITH RECURSIVE generate_rows(i, start_val, counter_val, end_val) AS (
    SELECT 1, 120, 1, 300 
    UNION ALL 
    SELECT i+1, 120, counter_val+1, 300 FROM generate_rows WHERE i < end_val
) 
INSERT INTO table1 (start, counter, difference, end) 
SELECT start_val, counter_val, start_val+counter_val, end_val FROM generate_rows;

-- Corrected logic to duplicate the rows
-- We calculate the current count and the next ID.
-- We then generate rows from (current_count + 1) to (2 * current_count).
-- This ensures that after insertion, the total row count is exactly double the original.
WITH RECURSIVE 
row_count AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) as cnt FROM table1
),
next_id AS (
    SELECT COALESCE(MAX(id) + 1, 1) as next_val FROM table1
),
generate_more(i, start_val, counter_val, end_val) AS (
    -- Anchor member: Start at the first new index (current count + 1)
    -- end_val is set to 2 * current_count to ensure we generate exactly 'cnt' new rows
    SELECT 
        (SELECT cnt FROM row_count) + 1, 
        (SELECT next_val FROM next_id), 
        1, 
        (SELECT cnt FROM row_count) * 2
    UNION ALL 
    -- Recursive member: Increment until we reach the target total count
    SELECT i+1, start_val, counter_val+1, end_val 
    FROM generate_more 
    WHERE i < end_val
) 
INSERT INTO table1 (start, counter, difference, end) 
SELECT start_val, counter_val, start_val+counter_val, end_val FROM generate_more;

-- Verify the results
SELECT * FROM table1

WITH time_points AS (
    -- 1. Get all unique timestamps from starts and ends
    SELECT start AS t FROM duration
    UNION
    SELECT "end" AS t FROM duration
),
ordered_times AS (
    -- 2. Sort the timestamps to create a sequence
    SELECT t FROM time_points ORDER BY t
),
intervals AS (
    -- 3. Create time slices by pairing each timestamp with the next one
    SELECT 
        t1.t AS interval_start,
        t2.t AS interval_end
    FROM ordered_times t1
    JOIN ordered_times t2 ON t2.rowid = t1.rowid + 1
    WHERE t2.t IS NOT NULL
)
-- 4. Count how many original durations overlap each interval
SELECT 
    interval_start,
    interval_end,
    (
        SELECT COUNT(*) 
        FROM duration d 
        WHERE d.start <= intervals.interval_start 
        AND d."end" > intervals.interval_start
    ) AS concurrent_count
FROM intervals
ORDER BY concurrent_count DESC, interval_start

WITH RECURSIVE atom_hierarchy AS (
    -- Anchor member: Start from the specific item 'TR007_8'
    SELECT 
        c.atom_id, 
        c.atom_id2, 
        c.bond_id, 
        1 AS level, 
        c.atom_id2 AS path -- Track the destination node to detect cycles
    FROM connected AS c
    WHERE c.atom_id = 'TR007_8'
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive member: Join to find the next connections
    SELECT 
        c.atom_id, 
        c.atom_id2, 
        c.bond_id, 
        ah.level + 1, 
        ah.path || ',' || c.atom_id2 -- Append the new destination to the path
    FROM connected AS c
    JOIN atom_hierarchy AS ah ON c.atom_id = ah.atom_id2
    -- Cycle prevention: Ensure the new destination has not been visited in the current path
    WHERE INSTR(ah.path, c.atom_id2) = 0
)
SELECT 
    atom_id, 
    atom_id2, 
    bond_id, 
    level 
FROM atom_hierarchy 
ORDER BY level, atom_id

SELECT

    -- Select all atoms
    SELECT 
        a.atom_id AS id, 
        a.molecule_id, 
        a.element AS val
    FROM atom AS a
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Select all bonds
    SELECT 
        b.bond_id AS id, 
        b.molecule_id, 
        b.bond_type AS val
    FROM bond AS b
) AS combined_data
LEFT JOIN (
    -- Subquery to get the composition of elements for each molecule
    SELECT 
        m.molecule_id, 
        GROUP_CONCAT(a.element, ',') AS elements
    FROM molecule AS m
    LEFT JOIN atom AS a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id
    GROUP BY m.molecule_id
) AS molecule_elements ON combined_data.molecule_id = molecule_elements.molecule_id
ORDER BY combined_data.id, combined_data.molecule_id

SELECT 
    m.id, 
    m.date, 
    CAST(ROUND(
        CASE 
            WHEN m.day_of_week BETWEEN 1 AND 5 THEN 
                MAX(0, 
                    (julianday(CASE 
                        WHEN julianday(m.match_end_time) < julianday(datetime(date(m.match_start_time) || ' 17:00:00')) 
                        THEN m.match_end_time 
                        ELSE datetime(date(m.match_start_time) || ' 17:00:00') 
                    END) - 
                    julianday(CASE 
                        WHEN julianday(m.match_start_time) > julianday(datetime(date(m.match_start_time) || ' 07:30:00')) 
                        THEN m.match_start_time 
                        ELSE datetime(date(m.match_start_time) || ' 07:30:00') 
                    END)
                ) * 1440
            ) 
            ELSE 0 
        END
    ) AS INTEGER) AS match_duration_minutes 
FROM (
    SELECT 
        id, 
        date, 
        CAST(strftime('%w', date) AS INTEGER) AS day_of_week, 
        datetime(date) AS match_start_time, 
        datetime(date, '+2 hours') AS match_end_time 
    FROM "match"
) AS m

WITH tiers AS (
    SELECT 1 AS from_hour, 4 AS to_hour, 20 AS pay
    UNION ALL SELECT 5, 8, 50
    UNION ALL SELECT 10, 12, 100
),
input_data AS (
    SELECT 6 AS user_hours
)
SELECT 
    i.user_hours, 
    SUM(
        t.pay * 
        CASE 
            WHEN i.user_hours < t.from_hour THEN 0
            WHEN i.user_hours BETWEEN t.from_hour AND t.to_hour THEN i.user_hours - t.from_hour + 1
            ELSE t.to_hour - t.from_hour + 1
        END
    ) AS sum_to_pay 
FROM input_data AS i 
CROSS JOIN tiers AS t

        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY home_team_api_id ORDER BY date) -

        MIN(date) AS start_date,

    GROUP BY home_team_api_id, grp

        TRIM(SUBSTR(remaining_dates, 1, INSTR(remaining_dates, ' ') - 1)) AS date_part

WITH 
-- 1. Define the universe of months based on the hardcoded dates provided in the original query
date_list AS (
    SELECT date(substr('12.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('12.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('12.01.2023', 1, 2)) AS act_date UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('19.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('19.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('19.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('19.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('19.01.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('12.02.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('12.02.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('12.02.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('31.01.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('31.01.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('31.01.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('13.02.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('13.02.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('13.02.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('17.02.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('17.02.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('17.02.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('17.02.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('17.02.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('17.02.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('19.02.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('19.02.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('19.02.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('10.03.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('10.03.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('10.03.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('12.03.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('12.03.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('12.03.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('27.03.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('27.03.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('27.03.2023', 1, 2)) UNION ALL
    SELECT date(substr('10.04.2023', 7, 4) || '-' || substr('10.04.2023', 4, 2) || '-' || substr('10.04.2023', 1, 2))
),

-- 2. Extract distinct months from the date list to form the time axis
months AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT 
        STRFTIME('%Y-%m', act_date) AS month_str,
        STRFTIME('%Y%m', act_date) AS month_sort
    FROM date_list
),

-- 3. Create the grid of all Activation Month vs Actual Month pairs
grid AS (
    SELECT 
        m1.month_str AS activation_month,
        m1.month_sort AS activation_sort,
        m2.month_str AS actual_month,
        m2.month_sort AS actual_sort
    FROM months m1
    JOIN months m2 ON m1.month_sort <= m2.month_sort
),

-- 4. Identify actual molecule activity based on the 'transaction' table
-- This maps real data to the time axis
molecule_activity AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        t.molecule_id,
        STRFTIME('%Y-%m', tr.date) AS activity_month_str,
        STRFTIME('%Y%m', tr.date) AS activity_month_sort
    FROM "transaction" tr
    JOIN "molecule" t ON tr.molecule_id = t.molecule_id
    WHERE STRFTIME('%Y-%m', tr.date) IN (SELECT month_str FROM months)
)

-- 5. Join the grid with activity data and count distinct molecules
SELECT 
    g.activation_month,
    g.actual_month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ma.molecule_id) AS active_molecules
FROM grid g
LEFT JOIN molecule_activity ma 
    ON ma.activity_month_sort >= g.activation_sort 
    AND ma.activity_month_sort <= g.actual_sort
GROUP BY g.activation_month, g.actual_month, g.activation_sort, g.actual_sort
ORDER BY g.activation_sort, g.actual_sort

WITH "credits" AS (
    SELECT 5001 AS "ID", 12 AS "ARTCRED", 7.5 AS "BIZCRED", 12 AS "HUMCRED", 14 AS "NATCRED", 11 AS "TEKCRED", 9 AS "GENCRED" 
    UNION ALL 
    SELECT 5002 AS "ID", 10.5 AS "ARTCRED", 6 AS "BIZCRED", 5 AS "HUMCRED", 4 AS "NATCRED", 6 AS "TEKCRED", 3 AS "GENCRED"
), 
"grp_choices" AS (
    SELECT 5001 AS "ID", 'ART' AS "GROUPCHOICE" 
    UNION ALL 
    SELECT 5001 AS "ID", 'BIZ' AS "GROUPCHOICE" 
    UNION ALL 
    SELECT 5001 AS "ID", 'NAT' AS "GROUPCHOICE" 
    UNION ALL 
    SELECT 5002 AS "ID", 'ART' AS "GROUPCHOICE"
) 
SELECT 
    G."ID", 
    G."GROUPCHOICE", 
    CASE 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'ART' THEN LC."ARTCRED" 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'BIZ' THEN LC."BIZCRED" 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'HUM' THEN LC."HUMCRED" 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'NAT' THEN LC."NATCRED" 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'TEK' THEN LC."TEKCRED" 
        WHEN G."GROUPCHOICE" = 'GEN' THEN LC."GENCRED" 
        ELSE 0 
    END AS "CREDIT" 
FROM "grp_choices" AS G
INNER JOIN "credits" AS LC ON G."ID" = LC."ID"
ORDER BY G."ID", G."GROUPCHOICE"

        'name' AS attribute_type,

    WHERE

    UNION ALL 
    SELECT 
        pa.player_api_id AS player_id,

        pa.preferred_foot AS attribute_value

WITH MY_TABLE AS (
    SELECT '1111' AS TECHNICAL_ID, 'NOTIONALCR' AS ASSET_TYPE 
    UNION ALL SELECT '1111', '50000' 
    UNION ALL SELECT '2222', 'FWDNOTLCR' 
    UNION ALL SELECT '2222', '50000' 
    UNION ALL SELECT '3333', '50000' 
    UNION ALL SELECT '3333', 'DUMMY'
), 
MAP_RECRF_ASSET_TYPE AS (
    SELECT 'SW' AS APPLICATION, 'NOTIONALCR' AS ASSET_TYPE, 1 AS PRIORITY 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'NOTIONALDB', 1 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'FWDNOTLCR', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'FWDNOTLDR', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'SWOFFBALCR', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'SWOFFBALDR', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'SWFWNOTLCR', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', 'SWFWNOTLDB', 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 'SW', '50000', 3
), 
CTE AS (
    SELECT 
        X.TECHNICAL_ID, 
        X.ASSET_TYPE, 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY X.TECHNICAL_ID ORDER BY MRAT.PRIORITY ASC) AS rn 
    FROM MY_TABLE AS X 
    INNER JOIN MAP_RECRF_ASSET_TYPE AS MRAT ON X.ASSET_TYPE = MRAT.ASSET_TYPE

SELECT valid_from, valid_until, count 
FROM (
    SELECT dt AS valid_from, 
           LEAD(dt) OVER (ORDER BY dt) AS valid_until, 
           SUM(change) OVER (ORDER BY dt) AS count 
    FROM (
        SELECT valid_from AS dt, 1 AS change 
        FROM (
            SELECT DATE('2014-01-01') AS valid_from, DATE('2014-01-31') AS valid_until 
            UNION ALL 
            SELECT DATE('2014-02-01') AS valid_from, DATE('2014-02-28') AS valid_until
        ) 
        UNION ALL 
        SELECT DATE(valid_until, '+1 day') AS dt, -1 AS change 
        FROM (
            SELECT DATE('2014-01-01') AS valid_from, DATE('2014-01-31') AS valid_until 
            UNION ALL 
            SELECT DATE('2014-02-01') AS valid_from, DATE('2014-02-28') AS valid_until
        )
    )
) 
WHERE count > 0 
  AND (valid_until IS NULL OR valid_from < valid_until)

WITH match_times AS (
    SELECT 
        match_id,
        -- Extract date part (YYYY-MM-DD)
        DATE(match_date) AS match_date_str,
        -- Extract time part and convert to fractional hours (e.g., 08:30 -> 8.5)
        CAST(SUBSTR(match_date, 12, 2) AS REAL) + 
        CAST(SUBSTR(match_date, 15, 2) AS REAL) / 60.0 AS hour_of_day
    FROM detail
),
target_times AS (
    SELECT 8 AS target_hour UNION ALL 
    SELECT 20 AS target_hour
),
distance_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        mt.match_date_str,
        mt.match_id,
        tt.target_hour,
        CASE 
            WHEN tt.target_hour = 8 THEN 'morning' 
            WHEN tt.target_hour = 20 THEN 'evening' 
        END AS time_period,
        ABS(mt.hour_of_day - tt.target_hour) AS time_distance,
        -- Rank matches by proximity to the target time for each day and period
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY mt.match_date_str, tt.target_hour 
            ORDER BY ABS(mt.hour_of_day - tt.target_hour)
        ) AS time_rank
    FROM match_times AS mt 
    CROSS JOIN target_times AS tt
)
SELECT 
    dc.match_date_str,
    dc.match_id,
    dc.time_period,
    dc.time_distance
FROM distance_calc AS dc 
WHERE dc.time_rank = 1 
ORDER BY dc.match_date_str, dc.target_hour

WITH my_table AS (
    SELECT 'A' AS cto, 1 AS nif, 'A1' AS file_, 5 AS filesize UNION ALL
    SELECT 'A' AS cto, 1 AS nif, 'A2' AS file_, 3 AS filesize UNION ALL
    SELECT 'A' AS cto, 2 AS nif, 'A3' AS file_, 2 AS filesize UNION ALL
    SELECT 'A' AS cto, 2 AS nif, 'A4' AS file_, 1 AS filesize UNION ALL
    SELECT 'B' AS cto, 1 AS nif, 'B1' AS file_, 4 AS filesize UNION ALL
    SELECT 'B' AS cto, 1 AS nif, 'B2' AS file_, 6 AS filesize
),
ordered_data AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cto ORDER BY file_) AS rn
    FROM my_table
),
group_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        -- Calculate the total size of the current identifier group
        SUM(filesize) OVER (PARTITION BY cto, nif) AS group_total_size,
        -- Calculate the running total of file sizes
        SUM(filesize) OVER (PARTITION BY cto ORDER BY file_ ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total,
        -- Calculate the running total of the previous row to determine the start of a partition
        SUM(filesize) OVER (PARTITION BY cto ORDER BY file_ ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS prev_running_total
    FROM ordered_data
),
partition_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        -- Determine if a new partition should start:
        -- 1. It is the first row in the category (rn = 1)
        -- 2. The previous running total was a multiple of 10 (meaning the previous partition was exactly full)
        -- 3. The previous running total was 0 (start of a new partition block)
        CASE 
            WHEN rn = 1 THEN 1
            WHEN prev_running_total = 0 THEN 1
            WHEN prev_running_total % 10 = 0 THEN 1
            ELSE 0
        END AS is_new_partition
    FROM group_stats
),
partition_groups AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        -- Calculate a cumulative sum of the is_new_partition flag to assign partition numbers
        SUM(is_new_partition) OVER (PARTITION BY cto ORDER BY file_) AS partition_num
    FROM partition_calc
)
SELECT 
    cto,
    nif,
    file_,
    partition_num,
    -- Calculate subpartition (row number within the partition)
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cto, partition_num ORDER BY file_) AS subpartition,
    -- Calculate total size of the partition
    SUM(filesize) OVER (PARTITION BY cto, partition_num) AS total_partition_size
FROM partition_groups
ORDER BY cto, file_

WITH RECURSIVE molecule_atoms_recursive AS (
    -- Base Case: Select each molecule and start the sequence at 1
    SELECT 
        molecule_id, 
        1 AS atom_number, 
        COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count 
    FROM atom 
    GROUP BY molecule_id
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Step: Increment the atom_number
    SELECT 
        mar.molecule_id, 
        mar.atom_number + 1, 
        mar.atom_count 
    FROM molecule_atoms_recursive AS mar
    -- Termination Condition: Stop when the number exceeds the count
    WHERE mar.atom_number < mar.atom_count
) 
SELECT molecule_id, atom_number 
FROM molecule_atoms_recursive 
ORDER BY molecule_id, atom_number

SELECT ItemName, ItemCount
FROM (
    -- Part 1: Get the Top 3 items
    SELECT 
        name AS ItemName, 
        COUNT(printings) AS ItemCount,
        0 AS SortOrder
    FROM cards
    GROUP BY name
    ORDER BY ItemCount DESC
    LIMIT 3
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Part 2: Get the sum of the rest
    SELECT 
        'Others' AS ItemName, 
        SUM(ItemCount) AS ItemCount,
        1 AS SortOrder
    FROM (
        -- Calculate counts for all items to filter out the top 3
        SELECT 
            name, 
            COUNT(printings) AS ItemCount,
            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(printings) DESC) AS rn
        FROM cards
        GROUP BY name
    ) AS AllItems
    WHERE rn > 3
) AS CombinedResults
ORDER BY SortOrder, ItemCount DESC

SELECT

    TA.chanceCreationCrossing,

    TA.defencePressure, 
    TA.defenceAggression, 
    TA.defenceTeamWidth 
FROM Team_Attributes AS TA
WHERE 
    TA.buildUpPlaySpeed > (SELECT AVG(CAST(buildUpPlaySpeed AS REAL)) FROM Team_Attributes WHERE buildUpPlaySpeed IS NOT NULL)
    AND TA.defenceTeamWidth < (SELECT AVG(CAST(defenceTeamWidth AS REAL)) FROM Team_Attributes WHERE defenceTeamWidth IS NOT NULL)
ORDER BY TA.buildUpPlaySpeed DESC, TA.defenceTeamWidth ASC

WITH TeamCounts AS (
    SELECT 
        team_api_id, 
        COUNT(*) AS record_count
    FROM Team_Attributes
    GROUP BY team_api_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
),
RankedAttributes AS (
    SELECT 
        TA.id,
        TA.team_api_id,
        TA.date,
        TA.buildUpPlaySpeed,
        TA.buildUpPlayPassing,
        TA.chanceCreationShooting,
        TA.defenceTeamWidth,
        -- Next record values (Lead)
        LEAD(TA.buildUpPlaySpeed) OVER (PARTITION BY TA.team_api_id ORDER BY TA.date) AS next_buildUpPlaySpeed,
        LEAD(TA.buildUpPlayPassing) OVER (PARTITION BY TA.team_api_id ORDER BY TA.date) AS next_buildUpPlayPassing,
        -- Previous record values (Lag)
        LAG(TA.chanceCreationShooting) OVER (PARTITION BY TA.team_api_id ORDER BY TA.date) AS prev_chanceCreationShooting,
        LAG(TA.defenceTeamWidth) OVER (PARTITION BY TA.team_api_id ORDER BY TA.date) AS prev_defenceTeamWidth
    FROM Team_Attributes TA
    INNER JOIN TeamCounts TC ON TA.team_api_id = TC.team_api_id
)
SELECT 
    id,
    team_api_id,
    date,
    buildUpPlaySpeed,
    buildUpPlayPassing,
    chanceCreationShooting,
    defenceTeamWidth,
    next_buildUpPlaySpeed,
    next_buildUpPlayPassing,
    prev_chanceCreationShooting,
    prev_defenceTeamWidth
FROM RankedAttributes
WHERE prev_defenceTeamWidth > buildUpPlaySpeed
ORDER BY team_api_id, date

LegalityStats AS (

items AS (

SELECT 
    molecule_id, 
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN element = 'na' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0) AS na_atoms, 
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE 
        WHEN element = 'c' AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM atom a2 WHERE a2.molecule_id = atom.molecule_id AND a2.element = 'cl') THEN 1 
        WHEN element = 'cl' AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM atom a2 WHERE a2.molecule_id = atom.molecule_id AND a2.element = 'c') THEN 1 
        WHEN element = 'c' AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM atom a2 WHERE a2.molecule_id = atom.molecule_id AND a2.element = 'cl' AND a2.atom_id < atom.atom_id) THEN 1 
        WHEN element = 'cl' AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM atom a2 WHERE a2.molecule_id = atom.molecule_id AND a2.element = 'c' AND a2.atom_id < atom.atom_id) THEN 1 
        ELSE 0 
    END), 0) AS c_atoms 
FROM atom 
GROUP BY molecule_id

WITH RankedDiagnoses AS (
    SELECT 
        ID, 
        "First Date" AS as_of_date, 
        Diagnosis AS current_diagnosis,
        LAG(Diagnosis) OVER (PARTITION BY ID ORDER BY "First Date") AS prev_diagnosis,
        CASE 
            WHEN Diagnosis IN ('RA susp.', 'PSS', 'SLE') THEN 1 
            ELSE 0 
        END AS is_target_diagnosis
    FROM Patient
),
FilteredChanges AS (
    SELECT 
        ID, 
        as_of_date, 
        current_diagnosis,
        is_target_diagnosis
    FROM RankedDiagnoses
    WHERE 
        -- Include the first record for the patient
        prev_diagnosis IS NULL 
        OR 
        -- Include records where the diagnosis changed
        current_diagnosis <> prev_diagnosis
)
SELECT 
    ID, 
    MIN(as_of_date) AS first_diagnosis_date
FROM FilteredChanges
WHERE is_target_diagnosis = 1
GROUP BY ID

    MAX(CASE WHEN RN = 2 THEN ELEMENT END) AS ELEMENT2,

FROM ATOMRNS

                INSTR(name || ' ', ' ') + 1,

    -- Extract the fourth word (from after the third space to the fourth space)

                INSTR(SUBSTR(name || ' ', INSTR(name || ' ', ' ') + INSTR(SUBSTR(name || ' ', INSTR(name || ' ', ' ') + 1), ' ') + INSTR(SUBSTR(name || ' ', INSTR(name || ' ', ' ') + INSTR(SUBSTR(name || ' ', INSTR(name || ' ', ' ') + 1), ' ') + 1), ' ') + 1), ' ') - 1)) AS name4

WITH hero_activity AS (
    SELECT 1 as id, '2023-01-01' as activity_date, 10 as activity_value, 1 as hero_id 
    UNION ALL SELECT 2, '2023-01-02', 20, 1 
    UNION ALL SELECT 3, '2023-02-01', 15, 1 
    UNION ALL SELECT 4, '2023-02-02', 25, 1 
    UNION ALL SELECT 5, '2023-01-03', 30, 2 
    UNION ALL SELECT 6, '2023-02-03', 40, 2
),
-- Step 1: Calculate the total activity value for each calendar month
monthly_sums AS (
    SELECT 
        strftime('%Y-%m', activity_date) AS activity_month,
        SUM(activity_value) AS month_total
    FROM hero_activity
    GROUP BY 1
),
-- Step 2: Use LAG to get the total of the previous month for each month row
previous_month_totals AS (
    SELECT 
        activity_month,
        month_total,
        LAG(month_total) OVER (ORDER BY activity_month) AS prev_month_total
    FROM monthly_sums
)
-- Step 3: Join back to the original data and calculate running totals
SELECT 
    ha.id,
    ha.activity_date,
    ha.activity_value,
    -- 1. Overall running total
    SUM(ha.activity_value) OVER (ORDER BY ha.activity_date) AS run_tot,
    -- 2. Monthly running total (resets every month)
    SUM(ha.activity_value) OVER (PARTITION BY strftime('%Y-%m', ha.activity_date) ORDER BY ha.activity_date) AS m_run_tot,
    -- 3. Previous month's total (joined from the CTE)
    COALESCE(pmt.prev_month_total, 0) AS required_output
FROM hero_activity ha
LEFT JOIN previous_month_totals pmt 
    ON strftime('%Y-%m', ha.activity_date) = pmt.activity_month
ORDER BY ha.activity_date

WITH RECURSIVE ExamSequence AS (
    -- Anchor Member: Select the first examination for each patient
    SELECT 
        ID, 
        "Examination Date", 
        1 AS seqnum, 
        'N' AS is_revisit, -- First visit is always 'N'
        "Examination Date" AS relevant_date -- The first date sets the initial relevant date
    FROM Examination e
    WHERE 
        -- Identify the first examination using ROW_NUMBER
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ID ORDER BY "Examination Date") = 1
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Member: Process subsequent examinations
    SELECT 
        e.ID, 
        e."Examination Date", 
        es.seqnum + 1, 
        -- Logic: If current date is > 1 year from relevant_date, mark 'Y', else 'N'
        CASE 
            WHEN e."Examination Date" > date(es.relevant_date, '+1 year') THEN 'Y' 
            ELSE 'N' 
        END AS is_revisit,
        -- Logic: If gap > 1 year, update relevant_date to current date, else keep old relevant_date
        CASE 
            WHEN e."Examination Date" > date(es.relevant_date, '+1 year') THEN e."Examination Date" 
            ELSE es.relevant_date 
        END AS relevant_date
    FROM Examination e
    INNER JOIN ExamSequence es ON e.ID = es.ID
    WHERE 
        -- Join condition: Find the next examination chronologically for the patient
        e."Examination Date" > es."Examination Date"
        -- Ensure we pick the immediate next examination to maintain sequence
        AND NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT 1 
            FROM Examination e_next 
            WHERE e_next.ID = e.ID 
              AND e_next."Examination Date" > es."Examination Date" 
              AND e_next."Examination Date" < e."Examination Date"
        )
)
SELECT 
    ID, 
    "Examination Date", 
    seqnum, 
    is_revisit
FROM ExamSequence
ORDER BY ID, "Examination Date"

-- SQLite does not have a built-in json_valid function to check for syntax errors.
-- To safely handle potentially invalid JSON strings, we can use a Common Table Expression (CTE)
-- that attempts to parse the string using json_extract. If the string is invalid, 
-- json_extract returns NULL, allowing us to filter out or identify invalid rows safely.

-- This query demonstrates the logic using the 'client' table, which contains a 'gender' column.
-- It constructs a JSON object for each client, and then attempts to update the 'gender' 
-- value from 'F' to 'M' only if the JSON is valid and the condition is met.

WITH 
-- 1. Construct sample JSON strings from the client table
sample_data AS (
    SELECT 
        '{"id": ' || CAST(client_id AS TEXT) || ', "gender": "' || gender || '"}' AS json_text
    FROM client
    LIMIT 5
),
-- 2. Attempt to parse the JSON. json_extract returns NULL if the input is not valid JSON.
-- This effectively acts as our safety check for invalid JSON.
parsed_data AS (
    SELECT 
        json_text,
        json_extract(json_text, '$.gender') AS current_gender
    FROM sample_data
)
-- 3. Apply the update logic: 
-- If the parsed gender is 'F', use json_set to change it to 'M'.
-- Otherwise (invalid JSON, key missing, or gender != 'F'), return the original text.
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN current_gender = 'F' THEN json_set(json_text, '$.gender', 'M')
        ELSE json_text 
    END AS info
FROM parsed_data

WITH RECURSIVE split_string AS (
    -- 1. Base Case: Select the first value before the first comma (or the whole string if no comma)
    SELECT 
        CAST(
            TRIM(
                SUBSTR('1.25,5.34,6.9', 1, 
                    INSTR('1.25,5.34,6.9', ',') - 1
                )
            ) AS REAL
        ) AS decimal_value,
        -- Calculate the starting position for the next iteration
        CASE 
            WHEN INSTR('1.25,5.34,6.9', ',') > 0 
            THEN INSTR('1.25,5.34,6.9', ',') + 1 
            ELSE 0 
        END AS next_pos,
        -- Keep the original string for reference in recursive steps
        '1.25,5.34,6.9' AS original_string
        
    UNION ALL
    
    -- 2. Recursive Step: Process the remainder of the string
    SELECT 
        CAST(
            TRIM(
                SUBSTR(original_string, next_pos, 
                    CASE 
                        WHEN INSTR(SUBSTR(original_string, next_pos), ',') > 0 
                        THEN INSTR(SUBSTR(original_string, next_pos), ',') - 1 
                        ELSE LENGTH(original_string) 
                    END
                )
            ) AS REAL
        ) AS decimal_value,
        -- Update the starting position for the next iteration
        CASE 
            WHEN INSTR(SUBSTR(original_string, next_pos), ',') > 0 
            THEN next_pos + INSTR(SUBSTR(original_string, next_pos), ',') 
            ELSE 0 
        END AS next_pos,
        original_string
    FROM split_string
    WHERE next_pos > 0
)
SELECT decimal_value 
FROM split_string

WITH merged_blackouts AS (
    -- 1. Merge overlapping or adjacent blackout periods into single continuous ranges
    SELECT 
        start_date AS mb_start, 
        end_date AS mb_end
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            start_date, 
            end_date,
            MAX(prev_end) OVER (ORDER BY start_date) AS max_prev_end
        FROM (
            SELECT 
                start_date, 
                end_date,
                LAG(end_date) OVER (ORDER BY start_date) AS prev_end
            FROM blackout_periods
        )
    ) 
    -- Keep only the start of a new merged block (where the current start is after the previous end)
    WHERE prev_end IS NULL OR start_date > max_prev_end
),
active_periods AS (
    SELECT start_date, end_date FROM account_activity
),
gaps AS (
    -- 2. Generate the 'clear' intervals by subtracting merged blackouts from active periods
    SELECT 
        a.start_date,
        a.end_date,
        b.mb_start,
        b.mb_end
    FROM active_periods a
    LEFT JOIN merged_blackouts b 
        ON b.mb_start < a.end_date 
        AND b.mb_end > a.start_date
),
split_points AS (
    -- 3. Collect all relevant boundary points to reconstruct the final intervals
    SELECT start_date AS pt FROM gaps
    UNION
    SELECT end_date AS pt FROM gaps
    UNION
    SELECT COALESCE(mb_start, start_date) AS pt FROM gaps
    UNION
    SELECT COALESCE(mb_end, end_date) AS pt FROM gaps
),
intervals AS (
    -- 4. Form intervals from consecutive points
    SELECT 
        pt AS start_date,
        LEAD(pt) OVER (ORDER BY pt) AS end_date
    FROM split_points
)
-- 5. Filter to keep only intervals that are valid (start < end) and actually fall within an active period
SELECT i.start_date, i.end_date
FROM intervals i
WHERE i.end_date IS NOT NULL
  AND i.start_date < i.end_date
  AND EXISTS (
      SELECT 1 
      FROM active_periods a 
      WHERE i.start_date >= a.start_date 
        AND i.end_date <= a.end_date
  )
ORDER BY i.start_date

SELECT 
    DriverId, 
    ContractType, 
    MIN(StartDate) AS StartDate, 
    MAX(EndDate) AS EndDate 
FROM (
    SELECT 
        DriverId, 
        ContractType, 
        StartDate, 
        EndDate,
        -- Calculate a Group ID to identify continuous blocks.
        -- We sum the days difference between the current StartDate and the previous EndDate.
        -- If dates overlap or are consecutive (diff <= 0), the sum doesn't increase, keeping them in the same group.
        -- If there is a gap (diff > 0), the sum increases, creating a new group ID.
        SUM(
            CASE 
                WHEN LAG(EndDate) OVER (
                    PARTITION BY DriverId, ContractType 
                    ORDER BY StartDate
                ) >= StartDate THEN 0 -- Overlap
                WHEN StartDate - DATE(LAG(EndDate) OVER (
                    PARTITION BY DriverId, ContractType 
                    ORDER BY StartDate
                )) > 1 THEN 1 -- Gap exists
                ELSE 0 -- Consecutive or First Row
            END
        ) OVER (
            PARTITION BY DriverId, ContractType 
            ORDER BY StartDate
        ) AS GroupId
    FROM driverContracts
) AS SubQuery
GROUP BY DriverId, ContractType, GroupId
ORDER BY DriverId, StartDate

WITH RECURSIVE CTE_RN AS (
    SELECT 
        e.exp_id, 
        e.mid, 
        e.qty, 
        i.qty AS LimitQty, 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY e.exp_id) AS rn 
    FROM Experiment AS e 
    INNER JOIN Inventory AS i ON e.mid = i.mid 
    WHERE e.mid = 2
), 
CTE_Recursive AS (
    -- Anchor Member: Process the first experiment
    SELECT 
        r.exp_id, 
        r.mid, 
        r.qty, 
        r.LimitQty, 
        r.rn,
        -- Initialize ConsumedQty and TotalConsumed
        CASE WHEN r.qty <= r.LimitQty THEN r.qty ELSE 0 END AS ConsumedQty,
        CASE WHEN r.qty <= r.LimitQty THEN r.qty ELSE 0 END AS TotalConsumed,
        CASE WHEN r.qty <= r.LimitQty THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS Executable
    FROM CTE_RN AS r 
    WHERE r.rn = 1
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive Member: Process subsequent experiments
    SELECT 
        r.exp_id, 
        r.mid, 
        r.qty, 
        r.LimitQty, 
        r.rn,
        -- Calculate ConsumedQty for this step based on remaining stock
        CASE WHEN r.qty <= (r.LimitQty - rec.TotalConsumed) THEN r.qty ELSE 0 END AS ConsumedQty,
        -- Update TotalConsumed: Previous Total + Current Consumed
        rec.TotalConsumed + CASE WHEN r.qty <= (r.LimitQty - rec.TotalConsumed) THEN r.qty ELSE 0 END AS TotalConsumed,
        CASE WHEN r.qty <= (r.LimitQty - rec.TotalConsumed) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS Executable
    FROM CTE_RN AS r 
    INNER JOIN CTE_Recursive AS rec ON rec.rn + 1 = r.rn
) 
SELECT exp_id, mid, Executable 
FROM CTE_Recursive 
ORDER BY rn

WITH RECURSIVE vt AS (
    SELECT 
        t.*, 
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY StartDate) AS seqnum 
    FROM transactions_1k AS t
),
cte AS (
    -- Anchor member: First transaction for each customer is always unflagged (0)
    SELECT 
        vt.TransactionID, 
        vt.CustomerID, 
        vt.StartDate, 
        vt.EndDate, 
        0 AS flag,
        vt.EndDate AS last_unflagged_end_date, -- Initialize reference date with the first transaction's EndDate
        vt.seqnum
    FROM vt 
    WHERE seqnum = 1
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive member: Process subsequent transactions
    SELECT 
        vt.TransactionID, 
        vt.CustomerID, 
        vt.StartDate, 
        vt.EndDate, 
        -- Flag as 1 if within 90 days of the last unflagged transaction's EndDate, else 0
        CASE 
            WHEN vt.StartDate <= date(cte.last_unflagged_end_date, '+90 days') THEN 1 
            ELSE 0 
        END AS flag,
        -- Update the reference EndDate: if current transaction is unflagged (0), use its EndDate; otherwise keep the previous reference
        CASE 
            WHEN vt.StartDate <= date(cte.last_unflagged_end_date, '+90 days') THEN cte.last_unflagged_end_date
            ELSE vt.EndDate 
        END AS last_unflagged_end_date,
        vt.seqnum
    FROM cte 
    JOIN vt ON vt.seqnum = cte.seqnum + 1 AND vt.CustomerID = cte.CustomerID
)
SELECT 
    TransactionID, 
    CustomerID, 
    StartDate, 
    EndDate, 
    flag 
FROM cte 
ORDER BY CustomerID, StartDate

WITH RECURSIVE Hierarchy AS (

    -- Recursive Member: Join to find the parent of the current record

    FROM staff s
    JOIN Hierarchy h ON s.id = h.parent_id

-- Final Selection: Filter to keep only the root nodes (where parent_id is NULL)
SELECT id AS ultimate_top_level_id
FROM Hierarchy

WITH filled_trans AS (
    SELECT 
        trans_id,
        -- Use LAST_VALUE with IGNORE NULLS to carry forward the last non-null value
        -- The frame 'ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW' ensures it looks at all previous rows
        LAST_VALUE(operation) IGNORE NULLS OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS new_operation,
        LAST_VALUE(k_symbol) IGNORE NULLS OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS new_k_symbol,
        LAST_VALUE(bank) IGNORE NULLS OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS new_bank,
        LAST_VALUE(account) IGNORE NULLS OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS new_account
    FROM trans
)
UPDATE trans
SET 
    operation = ft.new_operation,
    k_symbol = ft.new_k_symbol,
    bank = ft.new_bank,
    account = ft.new_account
FROM filled_trans ft
WHERE trans.trans_id = ft.trans_id
  AND (trans.operation IS NULL 
       OR trans.k_symbol IS NULL 
       OR trans.bank IS NULL 
       OR trans.account IS NULL)

-- Note: The 'card' table mentioned in the issue description is not present in the provided database schema. 
-- The logic below assumes the existence of a 'card' table with columns 'card_id', 'disp_id', and 'type'.
-- This operation requires two steps to handle the reordering logic correctly.

-- Step 1: Temporarily set the target disp_id to a value outside the existing range (e.g., 0 or a negative number) 
-- to avoid unique constraint violations and free up the target ID.
UPDATE card 
SET disp_id = 0 
WHERE disp_id = 41;

-- Step 2: Increment the disp_id of all cards in the same 'type' that have a disp_id >= 1.
-- This shifts the existing IDs to make room for the new ID.
UPDATE card 
SET disp_id = disp_id + 1 
WHERE disp_id >= 1 
  AND type = (SELECT type FROM card WHERE disp_id = 0);

-- Step 3: Finally, update the target card to the desired disp_id (1).
UPDATE card 
SET disp_id = 1 
WHERE disp_id = 0

SELECT 
    begin_date, 
    end_date, 
    (
        -- Calculate total months between start of begin_date and start of end_date
        (CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) - CAST(strftime('%Y', begin_date) AS INTEGER)) * 12 
        + CAST(strftime('%m', end_date) AS INTEGER) - CAST(strftime('%m', begin_date) AS INTEGER)
        - 1 -- Exclude the end month (partial)
        + CASE 
            -- If begin_date is the 1st, the start month is potentially full
            WHEN strftime('%d', begin_date) = '01' THEN 1 
            ELSE 0 
          END
        + CASE 
            -- If end_date is the last day of the month, the end month is full
            WHEN strftime('%d', end_date) = strftime('%d', date(end_date, 'start of month', '+1 month', '-1 day')) THEN 1

          END
    ) AS calculated_months

WITH RECURSIVE user_groups AS (
    -- Base case: Select each user and their initial ref1 and ref2 as separate groups
    SELECT 
        username, 
        ref1 AS ref, 
        ref1 AS root_ref
    FROM users
    WHERE ref1 IS NOT NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    SELECT 
        username, 
        ref2 AS ref, 
        ref2 AS root_ref
    FROM users
    WHERE ref2 IS NOT NULL
),
merged_groups AS (
    -- Recursive case: Merge groups if they share a reference
    SELECT 
        username, 
        ref, 
        root_ref
    FROM user_groups
    
    UNION ALL
    
    SELECT 
        ug.username, 
        ug.ref, 
        mg.root_ref
    FROM user_groups ug
    JOIN merged_groups mg ON ug.ref = mg.root_ref
    WHERE ug.root_ref <> mg.root_ref
),
final_groups AS (
    -- Deduplicate and find the minimum root_ref for each user to represent the merged group
    SELECT 
        username, 
        MIN(root_ref) AS group_ref
    FROM merged_groups
    GROUP BY username
),
asset_info AS (
    -- Count assets and collect IDs for each group
    SELECT 
        fg.group_ref, 
        GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT fg.username) AS usernames, 
        COUNT(DISTINCT a.id) AS asset_count, 
        GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT a.id) AS asset_ids
    FROM final_groups fg
    LEFT JOIN assets a ON a.owner = fg.group_ref
    GROUP BY fg.group_ref
)
-- Select all results, including users with no assets (propagated owner)
SELECT 
    group_ref AS owner, 
    usernames, 
    COALESCE(asset_count, 0) AS asset_count, 
    asset_ids
FROM asset_info

WITH combined AS (
    SELECT id, subgroup1 AS subgroup FROM superhero_group WHERE subgroup1 IS NOT NULL
    UNION
    SELECT id, subgroup2 AS subgroup FROM superhero_group WHERE subgroup2 IS NOT NULL
),
direct_connections AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        c1.id AS id1,
        c2.id AS id2
    FROM combined c1
    JOIN combined c2 ON c1.subgroup = c2.subgroup
    WHERE c1.id < c2.id
),
transitive_closure AS (
    -- Base case: direct connections
    SELECT id1, id2 FROM direct_connections
    UNION ALL
    -- Recursive step: find connections via intermediate nodes
    SELECT tc.id1, dc.id2
    FROM transitive_closure tc
    JOIN direct_connections dc ON tc.id2 = dc.id1
    WHERE NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM transitive_closure tc2
        WHERE tc2.id1 = tc.id1 AND tc2.id2 = dc.id2
    )
),
all_connections AS (
    -- Include self-connections for items that might not have links
    SELECT id AS id1, id AS id2 FROM superhero_group
    UNION
    SELECT id1, id2 FROM transitive_closure
),
groups AS (
    SELECT id1, MIN(id2) OVER (PARTITION BY id1) AS group_id
    FROM all_connections
)
SELECT DISTINCT id, group_id
FROM groups
ORDER BY id

WITH RECURSIVE split_tokens AS (
    -- 1. Base Case: Select the initial text and find the first token
    SELECT
        id,
        text,
        -- Extract the first token found between {{ and }}
        SUBSTR(
            text,
            INSTR(text, '{{'),
            INSTR(SUBSTR(text, INSTR(text, '{{')), '}}') + 1
        ) AS token,
        -- Calculate the position after the first token to continue searching
        INSTR(text, '{{') + INSTR(SUBSTR(text, INSTR(text, '{{')), '}}') + 1 AS next_pos
    FROM cards
    WHERE text LIKE '%{{%}}%'

    UNION ALL

    -- 2. Recursive Case: Find subsequent tokens in the remaining string
    SELECT
        id,
        text,
        -- Extract the next token from the substring starting at next_pos
        SUBSTR(
            SUBSTR(text, next_pos),
            INSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), '{{'),
            INSTR(SUBSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), INSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), '{{')), '}}') + 1
        ) AS token,
        -- Update next_pos for the next iteration
        next_pos + INSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), '{{') + INSTR(SUBSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), INSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), '{{')), '}}') + 1
    FROM split_tokens
    -- Continue recursion as long as there is another '{{' in the remaining text
    WHERE next_pos <= LENGTH(text) AND INSTR(SUBSTR(text, next_pos), '{{') > 0
)
-- 3. Final Selection: Group by ID to aggregate all tokens into a single list and count them
SELECT
    id,
    text,
    GROUP_CONCAT(token, ', ') AS bracketed_tokens,
    COUNT(token) AS token_count
FROM split_tokens
GROUP BY id, text
